speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: Most honored Timon,

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: It hath pleased the gods to remember my father’s age

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: And call him to long peace.

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: He is gone happy and has left me rich.

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: Then, as in grateful virtue I am bound

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: To your free heart, I do return those talents,

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: Doubled with thanks and service, from whose help

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: I derived liberty.

speaker: TIMON
text: O, by no means,

speaker: TIMON
text: Honest Ventidius. You mistake my love.

speaker: TIMON
text: I gave it freely ever, and there’s none

speaker: TIMON
text: Can truly say he gives if he receives.

speaker: TIMON
text: If our betters play at that game, we must not dare

speaker: TIMON
text: To imitate them. Faults that are rich are fair.

speaker: VENTIDIUS
text: A noble spirit!

speaker: TIMON
text: Nay, my lords, ceremony was but devised at first

speaker: TIMON
text: To set a gloss on faint deeds, hollow welcomes,

speaker: TIMON
text: Recanting goodness, sorry ere ’tis shown;

speaker: TIMON
text: But where there is true friendship, there needs none.

speaker: TIMON
text: Pray, sit. More welcome are you to my fortunes

speaker: TIMON
text: Than my fortunes to me.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: My lord, we always have confessed it.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Ho, ho, “confessed it”? Hanged it, have you not?

speaker: TIMON
text: O Apemantus, you are welcome.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: No, you shall not make me welcome.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: I come to have thee thrust me out of doors.

speaker: TIMON
text: Fie, thou ’rt a churl. You’ve got a humor there

speaker: TIMON
text: Does not become a man. ’Tis much to blame.—

speaker: TIMON
text: They say, my lords, Ira furor brevis est, but yond

speaker: TIMON
text: man is ever angry. Go, let him have a table by

speaker: TIMON
text: himself, for he does neither affect company, nor is

speaker: TIMON
text: he fit for ’t indeed.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Let me stay at thine apperil, Timon. I

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: come to observe; I give thee warning on ’t.

speaker: TIMON
text: I take no heed of thee. Thou ’rt an Athenian,

speaker: TIMON
text: therefore welcome. I myself would have no power;

speaker: TIMON
text: prithee, let my meat make thee silent.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: I scorn thy meat. ’Twould choke me, for I

speaker: APEMANTUS
text:
should ne’er flatter thee.
                 
                (
                Apart
                .
                )
               O you gods,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: what a number of men eats Timon, and he sees ’em

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: not! It grieves me to see so many dip their meat in

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: one man’s blood; and all the madness is, he cheers

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: them up too.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: I wonder men dare trust themselves with men.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Methinks they should invite them without knives.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Good for their meat, and safer for their lives.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: There’s much example for ’t. The fellow that sits

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: next him, now parts bread with him, pledges the

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: breath of him in a divided draft, is the readiest

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: man to kill him. ’T ’as been proved. If I were a huge

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: man, I should fear to drink at meals,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Lest they should spy my wind-pipe’s dangerous notes.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Great men should drink with harness on their throats.

speaker: TIMON
text: My lord, in heart! And let the health go round.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Let it flow this way, my good lord.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: “Flow this way”? A brave fellow.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: He keeps his tides well. Those healths will make

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: thee and thy state look ill, Timon.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Here’s that which is too weak to be a sinner,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Honest water, which ne’er left man i’ th’ mire.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: This and my food are equals. There’s no odds.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Feasts are too proud to give thanks to the gods.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Immortal gods, I crave no pelf.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: I pray for no man but myself.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Grant I may never prove so fond

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: To trust man on his oath or bond,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Or a harlot for her weeping,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Or a dog that seems a-sleeping,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Or a keeper with my freedom,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Or my friends if I should need ’em.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Amen. So fall to ’t.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Rich men sin, and I eat root.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Much good dich thy good heart, Apemantus!

speaker: TIMON
text: Captain Alcibiades, your heart’s in the field now.

speaker: ALCIBIADES
text: My heart is ever at your service, my lord.

speaker: TIMON
text: You had rather be at a breakfast of enemies

speaker: TIMON
text: than a dinner of friends.

speaker: ALCIBIADES
text: So they were bleeding new, my lord,

speaker: ALCIBIADES
text: there’s no meat like ’em. I could wish my best

speaker: ALCIBIADES
text: friend at such a feast.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Would all those flatterers were

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: thine enemies, then, that then thou mightst kill

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: ’em and bid me to ’em.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Might we but have that happiness, my

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: lord, that you would once use our hearts, whereby

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: we might express some part of our zeals, we

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: should think ourselves forever perfect.

speaker: TIMON
text: O, no doubt, my good friends, but the gods

speaker: TIMON
text: themselves have provided that I shall have much

speaker: TIMON
text: help from you. How had you been my friends else?

speaker: TIMON
text: Why have you that charitable title from thousands,

speaker: TIMON
text: did not you chiefly belong to my heart? I have told

speaker: TIMON
text: more of you to myself than you can with modesty

speaker: TIMON
text: speak in your own behalf. And thus far I confirm

speaker: TIMON
text: you. O you gods, think I, what need we have any

speaker: TIMON
text: friends if we should ne’er have need of ’em? They

speaker: TIMON
text: were the most needless creatures living, should we

speaker: TIMON
text: ne’er have use for ’em, and would most resemble

speaker: TIMON
text: sweet instruments hung up in cases, that keeps

speaker: TIMON
text: their sounds to themselves. Why, I have often

speaker: TIMON
text: wished myself poorer that I might come nearer to

speaker: TIMON
text: you. We are born to do benefits. And what better or

speaker: TIMON
text: properer can we call our own than the riches of

speaker: TIMON
text: our friends? O, what a precious comfort ’tis to

speaker: TIMON
text: have so many, like brothers, commanding one

speaker: TIMON
text: another’s fortunes. O, joy’s e’en made away ere ’t

speaker: TIMON
text: can be born! Mine eyes cannot hold out water,

speaker: TIMON
text: methinks. To forget their faults, I drink to you.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Thou weep’st to make them drink,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Timon.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Joy had the like conception in our eyes

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: And, at that instant, like a babe sprung up.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Ho, ho! I laugh to think that babe a bastard.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              LORD
text: I promise you, my lord, you moved me much.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Much!

speaker: TIMON
text: What means that trump?

speaker: TIMON
text: How now?

speaker: SERVANT
text: Please you, my lord, there are certain ladies

speaker: SERVANT
text: most desirous of admittance.

speaker: TIMON
text: Ladies? What are their wills?

speaker: SERVANT
text: There comes with them a forerunner, my lord,

speaker: SERVANT
text: which bears that office to signify their pleasures.

speaker: TIMON
text: I pray, let them be admitted.

speaker: CUPID
text: Hail to thee, worthy Timon, and to all

speaker: CUPID
text: That of his bounties taste! The five best senses

speaker: CUPID
text: Acknowledge thee their patron, and come freely

speaker: CUPID
text: To gratulate thy plenteous bosom. There

speaker: CUPID
text: Taste, touch, all, pleased from thy table rise;

speaker: CUPID
text: They only now come but to feast thine eyes.

speaker: TIMON
text: They’re welcome all. Let ’em have kind admittance.

speaker: TIMON
text: Music, make their welcome!

speaker: LUCIUS
text: You see, my lord, how ample you’re beloved.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Hoy-day!

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: What a sweep of vanity comes this way.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: They dance? They are madwomen.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Like madness is the glory of this life

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: As this pomp shows to a little oil and root.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: We make ourselves fools to disport ourselves

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: And spend our flatteries to drink those men

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Upon whose age we void it up again

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: With poisonous spite and envy.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Who lives that’s not depravèd or depraves?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Who dies that bears not one spurn to their graves

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Of their friends’ gift?

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: I should fear those that dance before me now

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Would one day stamp upon me. ’T ’as been done.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Men shut their doors against a setting sun.

speaker: TIMON
text: You have done our pleasures much grace, fair ladies,

speaker: TIMON
text: Set a fair fashion on our entertainment,

speaker: TIMON
text: Which was not half so beautiful and kind.

speaker: TIMON
text: You have added worth unto ’t and luster,

speaker: TIMON
text: And entertained me with mine own device.

speaker: TIMON
text: I am to thank you for ’t.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LADY
text: My lord, you take us even at the best.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Faith, for the worst is filthy and

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: would not hold taking, I doubt me.

speaker: TIMON
text: Ladies, there is an idle banquet attends you.

speaker: TIMON
text: Please you to dispose yourselves.

speaker:
ALL
               
              LADIES
text: Most thankfully, my lord.

speaker: TIMON
text: Flavius.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: My lord?

speaker: TIMON
text: The little casket bring me hither.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text:
Yes, my lord.
                 
                (
                Aside
                .
                )
               More jewels yet?

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: There is no crossing him in ’s humor;

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Else I should tell him well, i’ faith I should.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: When all’s spent, he’d be crossed then, an he could.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: ’Tis pity bounty had not eyes behind,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: That man might ne’er be wretched for his mind.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Where be our men?

speaker: SERVANT
text: Here, my lord, in readiness.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Our horses.

speaker: TIMON
text: O my friends, I have one word

speaker: TIMON
text: To say to you. Look you, my good lord,

speaker: TIMON
text: I must entreat you, honor me so much

speaker: TIMON
text: As to advance this jewel. Accept it and wear it,

speaker: TIMON
text: Kind my lord.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: I am so far already in your gifts—

speaker: ALL
text: So are we all.

speaker: SERVANT
text: My lord, there are certain nobles of the Senate

speaker: SERVANT
text: Newly alighted and come to visit you.

speaker: TIMON
text: They are fairly welcome.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: I beseech your Honor,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Vouchsafe me a word. It does concern you near.

speaker: TIMON
text: Near? Why, then, another time I’ll hear thee.

speaker: TIMON
text: I prithee, let’s be provided to show them entertainment.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: I scarce know how.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SERVANT
text: May it please your Honor, Lord Lucius,

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SERVANT
text: Out of his free love, hath presented to you

speaker:
SECOND
               
              SERVANT
text: Four milk-white horses trapped in silver.

speaker: TIMON
text: I shall accept them fairly. Let the presents

speaker: TIMON
text: Be worthily entertained.

speaker: TIMON
text: How now? What news?

speaker:
THIRD
               
              SERVANT
text: Please you, my lord, that honorable

speaker:
THIRD
               
              SERVANT
text: gentleman Lord Lucullus entreats your company

speaker:
THIRD
               
              SERVANT
text: tomorrow to hunt with him and has sent your

speaker:
THIRD
               
              SERVANT
text: Honor two brace of greyhounds.

speaker: TIMON
text: I’ll hunt with him; and let them be received,

speaker: TIMON
text: Not without fair reward.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: What will this come to?

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: He commands us to provide, and give great gifts,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: And all out of an empty coffer.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Nor will he know his purse or yield me this—

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: To show him what a beggar his heart is,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Being of no power to make his wishes good.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: His promises fly so beyond his state

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: That what he speaks is all in debt; he owes

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: For ev’ry word. He is so kind that he

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Now pays interest for ’t. His land’s put to their books.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Well, would I were gently put out of office

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Before I were forced out.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Happier is he that has no friend to feed

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Than such that do e’en enemies exceed.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: I bleed inwardly for my lord.

speaker: TIMON
text: You do yourselves much wrong.

speaker: TIMON
text: You bate too much of your own merits.

speaker: TIMON
text:
(
                Offering
                 
                a
                 
                gift
                .
                )
                 
              Here, my lord, a trifle of our love.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: With more than common thanks I will receive it.

speaker:
THIRD
               
              LORD
text: O, he’s the very soul of bounty!

speaker: TIMON
text: And now I remember, my lord, you gave good

speaker: TIMON
text: words the other day of a bay courser I rode on. ’Tis

speaker: TIMON
text: yours because you liked it.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: O, I beseech you, pardon me, my lord, in that.

speaker: TIMON
text: You may take my word, my lord. I know no man

speaker: TIMON
text: Can justly praise but what he does affect.

speaker: TIMON
text: I weigh my friends’ affection with mine own.

speaker: TIMON
text: I’ll tell you true, I’ll call to you.

speaker:
ALL
               
              LORDS
text: O, none so welcome.

speaker: TIMON
text: I take all and your several visitations

speaker: TIMON
text: So kind to heart, ’tis not enough to give.

speaker: TIMON
text: Methinks I could deal kingdoms to my friends

speaker: TIMON
text: And ne’er be weary.—Alcibiades,

speaker: TIMON
text: Thou art a soldier, therefore seldom rich.

speaker: TIMON
text: It comes in charity to thee, for all thy living

speaker: TIMON
text: Is ’mongst the dead, and all the lands thou hast

speaker: TIMON
text: Lie in a pitched field.

speaker: ALCIBIADES
text: Ay, defiled land, my lord.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: We are so virtuously bound—

speaker: TIMON
text: And so am I to you.

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: So infinitely endeared—

speaker: TIMON
text: All to you.—Lights, more lights.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: The best of happiness, honor, and fortunes

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Keep with you, Lord Timon.

speaker: TIMON
text: Ready for his friends.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: What a coil’s here,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Serving of becks and jutting-out of bums!

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: I doubt whether their legs be worth the sums

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: That are given for ’em. Friendship’s full of dregs.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Methinks false hearts should never have sound legs.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: Thus honest fools lay out their wealth on court’sies.

speaker: TIMON
text: Now, Apemantus, if thou wert not sullen,

speaker: TIMON
text: I would be good to thee.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: No, I’ll nothing, for if I should be bribed

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: too, there would be none left to rail upon thee, and

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: then thou wouldst sin the faster. Thou giv’st so

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: long, Timon, I fear me thou wilt give away thyself

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: in paper shortly. What needs these feasts, pomps,

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: and vainglories?

speaker: TIMON
text: Nay, an you begin to rail on society once, I am

speaker: TIMON
text: sworn not to give regard to you. Farewell, and

speaker: TIMON
text: come with better music.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: So. Thou wilt not hear me now, thou shalt

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: not then. I’ll lock thy heaven from thee.

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: O, that men’s ears should be

speaker: APEMANTUS
text: To counsel deaf, but not to flattery!
